beats.label: Title
beats.speaker_notes: Chapter 7. Three reasonable definitions of fairness. One dataset. You must pick one — and the theorem says you cannot have all three.
beats.on_slide_text: AI Engineering · Chapter 07 Fairness metrics: choosing a definition and defending it Three reasonable definitions. One dataset. Pick one — and learn why you cannot keep all three.
beats.narration_text: Welcome to Chapter 7. Today is about a problem that looks like it should have a clean answer, and doesn't. We're going to write down three definitions of fairness, each one completely reasonable, the kind of thing you'd nod along to in a meeting, and then I'm going to show you that on a single dataset you cannot have all three at once. So the work isn't picking the fair one. The work is choosing, and then defending the choice.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Welcome to Chapter 7. Today is about a problem that looks like it should have a clean answer, and doesn't. We're going to write down three definitions of fairness, each one completely reasonable, the kind of thing you'd nod along to in a meeting, and then I'm going to show you that on a single dataset you cannot have all three at once. So the work isn't picking the fair one. The work is choosing, and then defending the choice.

beats.label: The problem
beats.speaker_notes: You build a binary classifier on a population with two groups. You want it to be fair, so you write down requirements. Each sounds like fairness.
beats.on_slide_text: The problem You built a classifier. You sit down to write “make it fair.” A yes/no model — loan approval, recidivism, a hiring screen — applied to a population with two groups, A and B . The model predicts; the world produces outcomes; you compare the two. You write down three requirements. Each one, on its own, sounds exactly like what fairness should mean. I have to tell you: you cannot satisfy all three at once when the groups have different base rates — and on real data, they almost always do.
beats.narration_text: Let's start concrete. You've built a yes-or-no model. Approve the loan or not, flag the defendant or not, advance the candidate or not. And it runs on a population with two groups. You want it to be fair, so you sit down to write what fair actually means. Here's the trap: you'll naturally write three requirements, and every one of them sounds exactly like fairness. The bad news, which I'm going to prove to you, is that when the two groups have different base rates, and in the real world they almost always do, those three requirements fight each other.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Let's start concrete. You've built a yes-or-no model. Approve the loan or not, flag the defendant or not, advance the candidate or not. And it runs on a population with two groups. You want it to be fair, so you sit down to write what fair actually means. Here's the trap: you'll naturally write three requirements, and every one of them sounds exactly like fairness. The bad news, which I'm going to prove to you, is that when the two groups have different base rates, and in the real world they almost always do, those three requirements fight each other.

beats.label: The impossibility
beats.speaker_notes: This is a theorem, proved independently in 2016 by Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan, and by Chouldechova. It is the structural fact of the chapter.
beats.on_slide_text: The impossibility theorem · 2016 Demographic parity, equalized odds, and calibration parity cannot all hold on the same dataset when base rates differ. Not a tooling limit. Not a data problem. A structural fact — proved independently by Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan, and by Chouldechova. The impossibility doesn’t let you avoid the question. It forces a choice — and choices in engineering have to be defended.
beats.narration_text: Now, this isn't a hand-wavy fairness-is-hard sentiment. It's a theorem, proved independently in 2016 by two different groups, Kleinberg, Mullainathan and Raghavan on one side, and Chouldechova on the other. It says these three criteria are mathematically incompatible whenever the base rates differ. It's not a limitation of your tooling, it's not dirty data you can scrub clean, it's structural. And notice what a theorem like this does to you. It doesn't let you dodge the question. It forces you to choose. And choices, in engineering, have to be defended.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Now, this isn't a hand-wavy fairness-is-hard sentiment. It's a theorem, proved independently in 2016 by two different groups, Klineberg, Mullynathan and Rahgavan on one side, and Shooldekova on the other. It says these three criteria are mathematically incompatible whenever the base rates differ. It's not a limitation of your tooling, it's not dirty data you can scrub clean, it's structural. And notice what a theorem like this does to you. It doesn't let you dodge the question. It forces you to choose. And choices, in engineering, have to be defended.

beats.label: Three definitions
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider.
beats.on_slide_text: Part one Three definitions Each captures something fairness intuitively requires. Each is a different statement about the model.
beats.narration_text: So let's meet the three. This is part one. I want you to sit with each of these and notice two things at once: each one captures something fairness genuinely seems to require, and yet each is a different statement about your model.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So let's meet the three. This is part one. I want you to sit with each of these and notice two things at once: each one captures something fairness genuinely seems to require, and yet each is a different statement about your model.

beats.label: Demographic parity — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Demographic parity, a.k.a. statistical parity. A statement about the model's OUTPUTS, before comparing to ground truth.
beats.on_slide_text: Metric 01 · Equation Demographic parity also called statistical parity — a statement about the model’s outputs P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=b) In plain terms The model says “yes” to the same fraction of group A and group B. Equal treatment in the prediction itself — measured before anyone checks who was actually right. Values claim Outcomes should be proportional regardless of base rates. Structural redress over conditional accuracy.
beats.narration_text: First one: demographic parity, sometimes called statistical parity. The thing to hold onto is that this is a statement purely about the model's outputs. It never looks at whether the model was actually right. It just says the model should say yes to the same fraction of group A as group B. Equal positive rates. Whatever the prediction is, hand it out evenly.
beats.tts_normalized_text: First one: demographic parity, sometimes called statistical parity. The thing to hold onto is that this is a statement purely about the model's outputs. It never looks at whether the model was actually right. It just says the model should say yes to the same fraction of group A as group B. Equal positive rates. Whatever the prediction is, hand it out evenly.

beats.label: Demographic parity — tangent
beats.narration_text: Before we move on, let's actually read it. The left side is how often the model says yes to group A; the right side, how often it says yes to group B; and the equals sign demands those two rates be the same — not close, equal. Y-hat is the model's prediction, a one or a zero; A is the group; little a and little b are the two groups; P just means the probability of. So picture a bank that approves loans at forty percent in neighborhood A but twenty-two percent in B — forty does not equal twenty-two, parity is violated, and group B is handed loans far less often regardless of who would actually repay. The value buried in here: outcomes should be equal regardless of base rates. That's demographic parity. Now, back to the three definitions.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Before we move on, let's actually read it. The left side is how often the model says yes to group A; the right side, how often it says yes to group B; and the equals sign demands those two rates be the same — not close, equal. Y-hat is the model's prediction, a one or a zero; A is the group; little a and little b are the two groups; P just means the probability of. So picture a bank that approves loans at forty percent in neighborhood A but twenty-two percent in B — forty does not equal twenty-two, parity is violated, and group B is handed loans far less often regardless of who would actually repay. The value buried in here: outcomes should be equal regardless of base rates. That's demographic parity. Now, back to the three definitions.

beats.label: Demographic parity — example
beats.speaker_notes: Real-world example: loan approvals. The prediction itself carries the consequence.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example A bank approves loans at 40% in one neighborhood and 18% in another. Demographic parity says: the loan — the thing with real consequences, a house, a business — should be handed out at the same rate to both groups, full stop. It does not care whether default rates differ underneath. The claim is that access to the resource should not track group membership, even if the historical numbers do. When you reach for parity, you are betting that the prediction’s payoff matters more than its conditional accuracy. That is a defensible bet — in some deployments. Not all.
beats.narration_text: Picture a bank. It approves loans in one neighborhood at forty percent, and in another at eighteen percent. Demographic parity looks at that and says the loan itself, the thing that buys a house or starts a business, should reach both groups at the same rate, full stop. It deliberately does not ask whether default rates differ between the groups. The claim is about the outcome people actually live with, not about the model's accuracy.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Picture a bank. It approves loans in one neighborhood at forty percent, and in another at eighteen percent. Demographic parity looks at that and says the loan itself, the thing that buys a house or starts a business, should reach both groups at the same rate, full stop. It deliberately does not ask whether default rates differ between the groups. The claim is about the outcome people actually live with, not about the model's accuracy.

beats.label: Equalized odds — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Equalized odds: equal TPR and equal FPR across groups. A statement about the model's ERRORS, conditional on the truth.
beats.on_slide_text: Metric 02 · Equation Equalized odds a statement about the model’s errors , conditional on the truth True-positive rate equal P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=b) False-positive rate equal P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=b) In plain terms The model makes its mistakes evenly . Among people who truly are positive, it catches the same share in both groups; among people who truly are negative, it falsely flags the same share. Values claim The cost of being wronged should be borne equally. Group membership should not change your risk of error.
beats.narration_text: Second: equalized odds. This one does look at the truth. It says, among the people who really are positive, the model should catch them at the same rate in both groups, that's equal true-positive rates. And among the people who really are negative, it should falsely flag them at the same rate, that's equal false-positive rates. So where demographic parity was about outputs, equalized odds is about errors, and specifically about spreading your errors evenly across the groups.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Second: equalized odds. This one does look at the truth. It says, among the people who really are positive, the model should catch them at the same rate in both groups, that's equal true-positive rates. And among the people who really are negative, it should falsely flag them at the same rate, that's equal false-positive rates. So where demographic parity was about outputs, equalized odds is about errors, and specifically about spreading your errors evenly across the groups.

beats.label: Equalized odds — tangent
beats.narration_text: Let's read it. The left side is, among the people who truly are positive, how often the model catches group A; the right side is the same catch-rate for group B; and equalized odds demands they match — plus the identical demand on the false-alarm rate among the truly negative. Watch the two Y's: capital Y is the real label, Y-hat is the model's guess — keep them apart. So if a hiring screen advances ninety percent of the qualified people in group A but only seventy percent in B, ninety doesn't equal seventy — violated — and qualified people in B get passed over. The value here: the errors must fall equally. That's equalized odds. Back to the metrics.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Let's read it. The left side is, among the people who truly are positive, how often the model catches group A; the right side is the same catch-rate for group B; and equalized odds demands they match — plus the identical demand on the false-alarm rate among the truly negative. Watch the two Y's: capital Y is the real label, Y-hat is the model's guess — keep them apart. So if a hiring screen advances ninety percent of the qualified people in group A but only seventy percent in B, ninety doesn't equal seventy — violated — and qualified people in B get passed over. The value here: the errors must fall equally. That's equalized odds. Back to the metrics.

beats.label: Equalized odds — example
beats.speaker_notes: Real-world example: a hiring screen. Who absorbs the cost of a wrong rejection?
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example A hiring screen rejects qualified candidates twice as often in one group. Two qualified applicants, equally capable, different groups. If the model’s false-rejection rate is higher for one of them, that group systematically absorbs more of the harm the model causes. Equalized odds says: whatever errors the model makes, distribute them evenly — a false “no” should not be more likely just because of which group you’re in. This is the metric to reach for when the error itself is the injury — a wrongful arrest flag, a wrongful denial — and you want no group bearing more of it than another.
beats.narration_text: Here's why that matters. Take a hiring screen that rejects qualified candidates twice as often in one group as the other. Two people, equally capable, different groups, and one of them is far more likely to be wrongly turned away. That group is quietly absorbing more of the harm the model does when it's wrong. Equalized odds is the criterion that says the cost of your mistakes has to fall evenly.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Here's why that matters. Take a hiring screen that rejects qualified candidates twice as often in one group as the other. Two people, equally capable, different groups, and one of them is far more likely to be wrongly turned away. That group is quietly absorbing more of the harm the model does when it's wrong. Equalized odds is the criterion that says the cost of your mistakes has to fall evenly.

beats.label: Calibration parity — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Calibration / predictive parity. The probabilities mean the same thing for everyone.
beats.on_slide_text: Metric 03 · Equation Calibration parity also called predictive parity — a statement about whether the probabilities are honest P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p, A=a) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p, A=b) = p \quad \forall p In plain terms When the model says “70%” , it means a 70% chance — and it means that identically for group A and group B. A score has one meaning, not a group-specific one. Values claim If you act on the score, its meaning must not depend on group membership.
beats.narration_text: Third: calibration, or predictive parity. This one is about whether the model's probabilities are honest. It says that when the model outputs a number, say seventy percent, that number has to mean an actual seventy percent chance, and it has to mean the same thing for everyone, regardless of group. A score is a promise about reality, and calibration is the demand that the promise hold equally for both groups.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Third: calibration, or predictive parity. This one is about whether the model's probabilities are honest. It says that when the model outputs a number, say seventy percent, that number has to mean an actual seventy percent chance, and it has to mean the same thing for everyone, regardless of group. A score is a promise about reality, and calibration is the demand that the promise hold equally for both groups.

beats.label: Calibration — tangent
beats.narration_text: Read it in words first. The left side is, among everyone the model scored p, how many in group A actually turn out positive; the right side, the same for B; and both must equal p itself, for every p. P-hat is the score the model emits; little p is a particular score level; the upside-down A means for all. So among people scored zero-point-eight, suppose eighty percent of group A repay but only sixty-five percent of B — then zero-point-eight means two different things, and the score is biased. The value here: the number must be equally honest for everyone. That's calibration. Back to the metrics.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Read it in words first. The left side is, among everyone the model scored p, how many in group A actually turn out positive; the right side, the same for B; and both must equal p itself, for every p. P-hat is the score the model emits; little p is a particular score level; the upside-down A means for all. So among people scored zero-point-eight, suppose eighty percent of group A repay but only sixty-five percent of B — then zero-point-eight means two different things, and the score is biased. The value here: the number must be equally honest for everyone. That's calibration. Back to the metrics.

beats.label: Calibration parity — example
beats.speaker_notes: Real-world example: a clinical risk score read uniformly by clinicians.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example A clinician reads “high risk: 0.8” and acts the same way every time. A hospital’s readmission model outputs a probability. The doctor doesn’t know — or want to know — the patient’s group when they read it. For that to be safe, 0.8 must mean 0.8 for everyone : among all patients scored 0.8, exactly 80% are readmitted, in each group. Calibration parity is what lets a downstream decision-maker treat the number uniformly without secretly needing a group-specific decoder. Reach for calibration when a human or system consumes the probability directly and must trust that its meaning is stable across groups.
beats.narration_text: Think about a doctor reading a hospital's readmission risk score. It says high risk, zero point eight, and the doctor acts on it. And crucially, the doctor doesn't know, and shouldn't need to know, which group the patient belongs to. For that to be safe, zero point eight has to mean zero point eight for everybody. The moment the same score means different things for different groups, you've quietly handed the clinician a biased instrument.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Think about a doctor reading a hospital's readmission risk score. It says high risk, zero point eight, and the doctor acts on it. And crucially, the doctor doesn't know, and shouldn't need to know, which group the patient belongs to. For that to be safe, zero point eight has to mean zero point eight for everybody. The moment the same score means different things for different groups, you've quietly handed the clinician a biased instrument.

beats.label: Three values claims
beats.speaker_notes: The three metrics side by side. Each is a coherent value. None is the obvious correct one.
beats.on_slide_text: Three metrics · three values claims Each metric is a different statement — and a different value. Demographic parity statistical parity Equal rate of positive predictions — the visible outputs . Claim: outcomes proportional, regardless of base rates. Equalized odds TPR / FPR parity Equal false- and true-positive rates — the errors . Claim: the cost of error should be borne equally. Calibration parity predictive parity A score means the same probability across groups — honest outputs. Claim: a score’s meaning must not depend on group. They look compatible. They are not.
beats.narration_text: So line them up. Demographic parity cares about the visible outputs. Equalized odds cares about who bears the errors. Calibration cares about whether the scores tell the truth. Three different statements, but really, three different values. And I want to be clear: none of these is the obviously correct one. Each is a coherent moral position, and that's exactly what makes the next part uncomfortable.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So line them up. Demographic parity cares about the visible outputs. Equalized odds cares about who bears the errors. Calibration cares about whether the scores tell the truth. Three different statements, but really, three different values. And I want to be clear: none of these is the obviously correct one. Each is a coherent moral position, and that's exactly what makes the next part uncomfortable.

beats.label: The arithmetic
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider. We are going to make the impossibility obvious with numbers.
beats.on_slide_text: Part two The arithmetic The impossibility is not something you take on authority. It becomes obvious once you see the numbers.
beats.narration_text: Part two. I don't want you to take the impossibility on my authority, or even on the authority of a theorem you haven't watched get worked out. So we're going to make it obvious with numbers. Once you see the arithmetic, you can't un-see it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Part two. I don't want you to take the impossibility on my authority, or even on the authority of a theorem you haven't watched get worked out. So we're going to make it obvious with numbers. Once you see the arithmetic, you can't un-see it.

beats.label: Base rates differ
beats.speaker_notes: Set up the numbers: base rate 0.6 in group A, 0.3 in group B. Calibrated scores in both.
beats.on_slide_text: The setup One number does all the damage: the base rate. Group A 0.6 60% truly positive Group B 0.3 30% truly positive The classifier is calibrated — among everyone it scores at probability p̂ , the realized positive rate really is p̂ , in both groups. Now watch what calibration forces.
beats.narration_text: Here's the whole setup, and it really is this simple. Group A has a base rate of sixty percent truly positive. Group B, thirty percent. One number, the base rate, does all of the damage. And we'll assume the classifier is calibrated, meaning that among everyone it scores at probability p, the real positive rate is p, honestly, in both groups. Now just watch what that one assumption forces.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Here's the whole setup, and it really is this simple. Group A has a base rate of sixty percent truly positive. Group B, thirty percent. One number, the base rate, does all of the damage. And we'll assume the classifier is calibrated, meaning that among everyone it scores at probability p, the real positive rate is p, honestly, in both groups. Now just watch what that one assumption forces.

beats.label: PPV — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Positive predictive value for calibrated scores. Precision among those flagged positive.
beats.on_slide_text: Equation · precision Among those flagged positive, how many are right? \text{PPV} = \frac{p \cdot \text{TPR}}{p \cdot \text{TPR} + (1-p)\cdot \text{FPR}} In plain terms Precision depends on the base rate p . Where positives are common (A, p=0.6), a flag is more likely to be correct. Where positives are rare (B, p=0.3), the same flag is more likely a false alarm. Why it bites To hold PPV equal across groups with different p , the ratio TPR/FPR must differ — which breaks equalized odds.
beats.narration_text: The quantity to track is positive predictive value, which is just precision. Of the people you flagged positive, how many actually were positive? And here's the key fact: precision depends on the base rate. Where positives are common, a flag is more likely to be a real one. Where positives are rare, that exact same flag is more likely to be a false alarm, even with an identical model.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The quantity to track is positive predictive value, which is just precision. Of the people you flagged positive, how many actually were positive? And here's the key fact: precision depends on the base rate. Where positives are common, a flag is more likely to be a real one. Where positives are rare, that exact same flag is more likely to be a false alarm, even with an identical model.

beats.label: Precision (PPV) — tangent
beats.narration_text: This one's a definition, so read what it builds. The left side is precision — of everyone the model flags positive, how many really are. The right side builds that from the base rate and the two error rates, and the thing to notice is the base rate p sitting right on top. So hold the model fixed — same true-positive and false-positive rates — and just change the base rate from zero-point-six to zero-point-three: precision falls from about zero-point-seven to about zero-point-four. Same model, far less precise where positives are rarer. That's the lever behind the whole impossibility. Back to the arithmetic.
beats.tts_normalized_text: This one's a definition, so read what it builds. The left side is precision — of everyone the model flags positive, how many really are. The right side builds that from the base rate and the two error rates, and the thing to notice is the base rate p sitting right on top. So hold the model fixed — same true-positive and false-positive rates — and just change the base rate from zero-point-six to zero-point-three: precision falls from about zero-point-seven to about zero-point-four. Same model, far less precise where positives are rarer. That's the lever behind the whole impossibility. Back to the arithmetic.

beats.label: PPV — example
beats.speaker_notes: Real-world example: the same alarm in two rooms with different prevalence.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example The same smoke alarm is “more trustworthy” in the kitchen than the bedroom. One detector, one sensitivity. Put it in a kitchen, where fires are relatively common, and most of its alarms are real. Put the identical detector in a bedroom, where fires are rare, and most of its alarms are false — even though the device hasn’t changed at all. Prevalence, not the device, sets how often a positive is correct. That is exactly why a calibrated classifier with different base rates cannot also equalize its error rates. The math you just saw is the smoke alarm.
beats.narration_text: The cleanest intuition I know for this is a smoke alarm. One detector, one sensitivity, fixed. Put it in the kitchen, where fires are relatively common, and most of its alarms are real. Take the identical alarm, put it in the bedroom, where fires are rare, and most of its alarms are false. Same device. The trustworthiness of the alarm changed because the base rate changed. Your classifier is that alarm, and your two groups are the two rooms.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The cleanest intuition I know for this is a smoke alarm. One detector, one sensitivity, fixed. Put it in the kitchen, where fires are relatively common, and most of its alarms are real. Take the identical alarm, put it in the bedroom, where fires are rare, and most of its alarms are false. Same device. The trustworthiness of the alarm changed because the base rate changed. Your classifier is that alarm, and your two groups are the two rooms.

beats.label: Chouldechova's identity
beats.speaker_notes: Chouldechova's identity from Bayes. The constraint is algebraically inescapable.
beats.on_slide_text: Equation · the inescapable constraint Chouldechova’s identity — straight from Bayes \frac{v}{1-v} = \frac{p}{1-p}\cdot \frac{t}{f} equal PPV across groups, with different base rates, requires… \frac{t_a}{f_a} = \frac{p_b(1-p_a)}{p_a(1-p_b)}\cdot \frac{t_b}{f_b} In plain terms Here v = precision, t = TPR, f = FPR, p = base rate. Unless p a = p b , the ratio t/f must differ across groups. But equalized odds demands t a =t b and f a =f b — forcing t/f equal. You cannot have both. One of them has to give.
beats.narration_text: You can write this down exactly, and Chouldechova did. It falls straight out of Bayes' rule: there's a fixed relationship between precision, the base rate, and your error rates. And once you stare at that relationship, the conclusion is forced. If two groups have different base rates, you cannot keep precision equal and keep your error rates equal at the same time. It's not a modeling choice. It's algebra. There's no threshold, no clever loss function, that gets you out of it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: You can write this down exactly, and Shooldekova did. It falls straight out of Bayz' rule: there's a fixed relationship between precision, the base rate, and your error rates. And once you stare at that relationship, the conclusion is forced. If two groups have different base rates, you cannot keep precision equal and keep your error rates equal at the same time. It's not a modeling choice. It's algebra. There's no threshold, no clever loss function, that gets you out of it.

beats.label: Chouldechova's identity — tangent
beats.narration_text: This is an identity — it falls out of Bayes and always holds, so read what it forces. The left side is the odds a flagged person truly is positive — that's just precision rewritten. The right side is the base-rate odds times the model's hit-to-false ratio. Because the equals sign always holds, if you demand equal precision across two groups with different base rates, the hit-to-false ratio is forced to differ between them. In plain terms: you cannot equalize precision and equalize the error rates at once. That's the algebra under the impossibility theorem. Back to it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: This is an identity — it falls out of Bayz and always holds, so read what it forces. The left side is the odds a flagged person truly is positive — that's just precision rewritten. The right side is the base-rate odds times the model's hit-to-false ratio. Because the equals sign always holds, if you demand equal precision across two groups with different base rates, the hit-to-false ratio is forced to differ between them. In plain terms: you cannot equalize precision and equalize the error rates at once. That's the algebra under the impossibility theorem. Back to it.

beats.label: The triangle
beats.speaker_notes: The theorem as a triangle: you can satisfy any two. The third breaks. Watch the cycle — it rotates through which two you keep and which one breaks.
beats.on_slide_text: The theorem, drawn Satisfy any two. The third breaks.
beats.narration_text: Here's the theorem as a picture. Three corners, the three criteria, and you can satisfy any two of them. Pick any pair you like, and the third one breaks. Watch it cycle. Keep calibration and equalized odds, parity goes. Keep parity and calibration, the odds go. There is no setting that holds all three. You are always choosing which one to give up.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Here's the theorem as a picture. Three corners, the three criteria, and you can satisfy any two of them. Pick any pair you like, and the third one breaks. Watch it cycle. Keep calibration and equalized odds, parity goes. Keep parity and calibration, the odds go. There is no setting that holds all three. You are always choosing which one to give up.

beats.label: Arithmetic made visible
beats.speaker_notes: Same model, two threshold choices. Toggle between the calibration-satisfying and equalized-odds-satisfying versions. Calibration and equalized odds cannot both hold while base rates differ.
beats.on_slide_text: The trade-off, in bars Same model. Different threshold. You choose what breaks.
beats.narration_text: And this is the same model, just two different threshold choices, drawn as bars. Move the threshold to satisfy calibration, and the error rates split apart between the groups. Move it to equalize the odds, and the calibration breaks. You're not fixing anything by sliding that threshold. You're only choosing what breaks. The trade-off is a steering wheel, not an escape hatch.
beats.tts_normalized_text: And this is the same model, just two different threshold choices, drawn as bars. Move the threshold to satisfy calibration, and the error rates split apart between the groups. Move it to equalize the odds, and the calibration breaks. You're not fixing anything by sliding that threshold. You're only choosing what breaks. The trade-off is a steering wheel, not an escape hatch.

beats.label: The COMPAS case
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider. The most famous instance of the theorem playing out in public.
beats.on_slide_text: Part three The COMPAS case Broward County, 2016. The theorem, in public, with real consequences.
beats.narration_text: Part three. Let's leave the abstract and go to the most famous time this theorem detonated in public. Broward County, Florida, 2016. A courtroom risk score, and a fight that, underneath all the headlines, was this exact theorem playing out, with real people's freedom on the line.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Part three. Let's leave the abstract and go to the most famous time this theorem detonated in public. Broward County, Florida, 2016. A courtroom risk score, and a fight that, underneath all the headlines, was this exact theorem playing out, with real people's freedom on the line.

beats.label: COMPAS — what happened
beats.speaker_notes: ProPublica found a higher FPR for Black defendants (equalized odds violated). Northpointe said the tool was calibrated. Both were right.
beats.on_slide_text: A risk score that decided bail and sentencing Two true claims. One values disagreement. ProPublica Black defendants who were not re-arrested were flagged high-risk about twice as often as white defendants who weren’t. → equalized odds violated Northpointe Within each risk score, the actual re-arrest rate was about the same across races. A 7 meant a 7 for everyone. → calibration parity satisfied Base rates of re-arrest differed in the data. The theorem says: given that, you cannot have both. They weren’t having a factual fight more data could settle — they were disagreeing about which definition of fairness should win .
beats.narration_text: The tool was called COMPAS, and it fed into bail and sentencing decisions. ProPublica investigated and found that Black defendants who were not re-arrested had been flagged high-risk about twice as often as white defendants who weren't. That's a false-positive-rate gap, a violation of equalized odds. Northpointe, the company, fired back: within any given risk score, the re-offend rate was about the same across groups, so the tool was calibrated. And here's the thing you have to absorb. They were both right.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The tool was called Compass, and it fed into bail and sentencing decisions. ProPublica investigated and found that Black defendants who were not re-arrested had been flagged high-risk about twice as often as white defendants who weren't. That's a false-positive-rate gap, a violation of equalized odds. North Point, the company, fired back: within any given risk score, the re-offend rate was about the same across groups, so the tool was calibrated. And here's the thing you have to absorb. They were both right.

beats.label: COMPAS — both were right
beats.speaker_notes: Left: the FPR gap ProPublica measured. Right: the calibration Northpointe measured — re-offend rate by score, nearly identical across groups. Both charts are true at once.
beats.on_slide_text: Both pictures are true at the same time The disagreement was never about the numbers.
beats.narration_text: Both pictures were true at the same time. ProPublica's chart showing the error gap, and Northpointe's chart showing nearly identical re-offend rates within each score. Neither side was lying, neither side was fudging numbers. The disagreement was never about the data. It was about which definition of fairness the tool owed people. That's the theorem, wearing a courtroom's clothes.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Both pictures were true at the same time. ProPublica's chart showing the error gap, and North Point's chart showing nearly identical re-offend rates within each score. Neither side was lying, neither side was fudging numbers. The disagreement was never about the data. It was about which definition of fairness the tool owed people. That's the theorem, wearing a courtroom's clothes.

beats.label: Each metric is a values claim
beats.speaker_notes: The chapter's main lesson. Each is a coherent value. None is the obvious correct one. The choice depends on the deployment.
beats.on_slide_text: The lesson Each metric is a coherent value. None is the obvious right one. The choice depends on the deployment: who uses the prediction, what decisions they make, who bears the cost of error , and whether the base-rate difference is itself something you have a stance on. The math cannot answer that for you.
beats.narration_text: So here's the lesson of the chapter, and if you take one thing away, take this. Each of these metrics is a coherent value, and none of them is the automatically-right one. Which one you owe depends on the deployment: who's using the prediction, what decision they make with it, who pays for the model's mistakes, and whether the base-rate difference itself is something you think is just or unjust. The math is settled. The choice is yours, and it is a moral one.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So here's the lesson of the chapter, and if you take one thing away, take this. Each of these metrics is a coherent value, and none of them is the automatically-right one. Which one you owe depends on the deployment: who's using the prediction, what decision they make with it, who pays for the model's mistakes, and whether the base-rate difference itself is something you think is just or unjust. The math is settled. The choice is yours, and it is a moral one.

beats.label: Individual fairness
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider. Group fairness is silent about individual cases. Aristotelian intuition: similar individuals should be treated similarly.
beats.on_slide_text: Part four Beyond groups: individual fairness A model can satisfy demographic parity while treating two nearly-identical people completely differently. Similar individuals should be treated similarly.
beats.narration_text: Part four. Everything so far has been about groups, and group fairness has a real blind spot. A model can satisfy demographic parity perfectly and still treat two nearly-identical people completely differently, as long as the group totals come out even. So let's bring in the older intuition, the Aristotelian one: similar individuals should be treated similarly.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Part four. Everything so far has been about groups, and group fairness has a real blind spot. A model can satisfy demographic parity perfectly and still treat two nearly-identical people completely differently, as long as the group totals come out even. So let's bring in the older intuition, the Aristoteelian one: similar individuals should be treated similarly.

beats.label: Lipschitz — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Dwork et al. 2012. The (D,d)-Lipschitz condition. The difference in treatment is bounded by the difference between individuals.
beats.on_slide_text: Equation · Dwork et al. 2012 The (D, d)-Lipschitz condition D\big(M(x), M(y)\big) \leq d(x, y) \quad \forall\, x, y \in \mathcal{X} In plain terms How differently the model treats two people (left) can be no bigger than how different the two people are (right). D measures distance between outputs; d measures distance between individuals. The whole game is d Who counts as “similar” for this task? That choice isn’t in the math — it precedes the math, and it’s a values decision.
beats.narration_text: Dwork and colleagues formalized this in 2012 with what's called a Lipschitz condition. In plain words: the difference in how the model treats two people can be no larger than how different those two people actually are. Close people get close treatment. The model isn't allowed to suddenly swing between two applicants who are basically the same. It's a smoothness requirement, placed on fairness itself.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Dwork and colleagues formalized this in 2012 with what's called a Lipshits condition. In plain words: the difference in how the model treats two people can be no larger than how different those two people actually are. Close people get close treatment. The model isn't allowed to suddenly swing between two applicants who are basically the same. It's a smoothness requirement, placed on fairness itself.

beats.label: Lipschitz condition — tangent
beats.narration_text: This one is an inequality, and the inequality is the whole idea. The left side is how differently the model treats two people; the right side is how different those two people actually are; and the less-than-or-equal sign says the treatment gap can be no bigger than the person gap. M is the model, big D is distance between its outputs, little d is distance between people. So take two applicants with identical finances and different ZIP codes — if your distance d ignores ZIP, then d is zero, and the model is forced to treat them identically. But if d counts ZIP as a real difference, unequal treatment quietly becomes allowed. The value: similar people should be treated similarly — and someone has to define similar. Back to it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: This one is an inequality, and the inequality is the whole idea. The left side is how differently the model treats two people; the right side is how different those two people actually are; and the less-than-or-equal sign says the treatment gap can be no bigger than the person gap. M is the model, big D is distance between its outputs, little d is distance between people. So take two applicants with identical finances and different ZIP codes — if your distance d ignores ZIP, then d is zero, and the model is forced to treat them identically. But if d counts ZIP as a real difference, unequal treatment quietly becomes allowed. The value: similar people should be treated similarly — and someone has to define similar. Back to it.

beats.label: Lipschitz — example
beats.speaker_notes: Two loan applicants, identical financials, different zip codes. The fairness guarantee is only as good as the similarity metric d. Bars: how much different treatment is permitted as the chosen d says two people are more different.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example Two applicants, identical finances, different ZIP codes. If d ignores ZIP, the model must treat them alike. If d encodes ZIP distance, different treatment is permitted — and structural inequity slips into the fairness condition itself.
beats.narration_text: Two loan applicants, identical finances, different ZIP codes. The whole guarantee now rests on one thing: your similarity metric, the function that decides how different two people really are. If that metric ignores ZIP, the model must treat them alike. But if it encodes ZIP as a genuine difference, then different treatment becomes permitted, and you've just smuggled structural inequity into the very condition that was supposed to protect against it. The guarantee is only as honest as the distance function underneath it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Two loan applicants, identical finances, different ZIP codes. The whole guarantee now rests on one thing: your similarity metric, the function that decides how different two people really are. If that metric ignores ZIP, the model must treat them alike. But if it encodes ZIP as a genuine difference, then different treatment becomes permitted, and you've just smuggled structural inequity into the very condition that was supposed to protect against it. The guarantee is only as honest as the distance function underneath it.

beats.label: Unawareness vs awareness
beats.speaker_notes: FTU vs FTA. Fairness through unawareness is intuitive but weak — proxies reconstruct the sensitive attribute. FTU is a documentation choice, not a guarantee.
beats.on_slide_text: A tempting shortcut that doesn’t work “Just delete the sensitive attribute.” Fairness through unawareness Drop race, gender, etc. from the model. Intuitive — and mathematically weak. ZIP code, surname, occupation are proxies that reconstruct the attribute. Omit race, keep ZIP, and you may still use race. Fairness through awareness Reason explicitly about the attribute — how much it enters d . Auditing means computing d and checking the Lipschitz condition, not confirming a column was deleted. Takeaway: unawareness is not a fairness guarantee. It is a documentation choice.
beats.narration_text: Now the tempting shortcut: just delete the sensitive attribute. Drop race, drop gender, and surely the model can't discriminate. This is called fairness through unawareness, and it is intuitive and mathematically weak. The reason is proxies. ZIP code, surname, occupation quietly reconstruct the attribute you deleted. Omit race but keep ZIP, and the model can still find race. Deleting the column is a documentation choice, not a guarantee.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Now the tempting shortcut: just delete the sensitive attribute. Drop race, drop gender, and surely the model can't discriminate. This is called fairness through unawareness, and it is intuitive and mathematically weak. The reason is proxies. ZIP code, surname, occupation quietly reconstruct the attribute you deleted. Omit race but keep ZIP, and the model can still find race. Deleting the column is a documentation choice, not a guarantee.

beats.label: Causal fairness
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider. Some fairness questions need a causal model of how the world generates the data — not just the data.
beats.on_slide_text: Part five Causal fairness Statistical metrics read the data. But to tell discrimination from a justified disparity, you need a model of how the data was generated.
beats.narration_text: Part five. Up to now, every metric just reads the data as it sits. But some fairness questions can't be answered from the data alone, because you have to distinguish discrimination from a disparity that's actually justified, and those two can produce data that looks identical. To tell them apart, you need a model of how the data was generated in the first place.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Part five. Up to now, every metric just reads the data as it sits. But some fairness questions can't be answered from the data alone, because you have to distinguish discrimination from a disparity that's actually justified, and those two can produce data that looks identical. To tell them apart, you need a model of how the data was generated in the first place.

beats.label: Three causal paths
beats.speaker_notes: A correlation between A and Y-hat can arise three ways: direct, indirect through a mediator, or spurious through a confounder. Observational metrics conflate all three.
beats.on_slide_text: Three ways A can reach the prediction Same correlation, three different causes. Disparate treatment is the direct path; disparate impact is the indirect one. The right policy response differs for each — and observational metrics cannot tell them apart.
beats.narration_text: A correlation between group and prediction can come about three different ways. It can be direct, where the group affects the decision straight up. It can be indirect, running through some legitimate mediator. Or it can be spurious, driven by a confounder sitting behind both. Disparate treatment is the direct path; disparate impact is the indirect one, and the right response is completely different for each. The uncomfortable part: your observational metrics can't tell these three apart. They see one correlation and conflate all three causes.
beats.tts_normalized_text: A correlation between group and prediction can come about three different ways. It can be direct, where the group affects the decision straight up. It can be indirect, running through some legitimate mediator. Or it can be spurious, driven by a confounder sitting behind both. Disparate treatment is the direct path; disparate impact is the indirect one, and the right response is completely different for each. The uncomfortable part: your observational metrics can't tell these three apart. They see one correlation and conflate all three causes.

beats.label: Counterfactual fairness — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Kusner et al. 2017. Would this individual get the same decision if they had belonged to a different group, all else equal? A do-calculus intervention at the individual level.
beats.on_slide_text: Equation · Kusner et al. 2017 Counterfactual fairness P\!\left(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U)=y \mid X=x, A=a\right) = P\!\left(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U)=y \mid X=x, A=a\right) In plain terms Would this exact person get the same decision if we flipped their group and held their background fixed? If yes, the prediction is counterfactually fair to them. 1 Abduction infer the latent background U from what you observe 2 Action intervene: set A to a′, hold U fixed 3 Prediction propagate forward, read the new Ŷ
beats.narration_text: Kusner and colleagues, in 2017, gave us a sharp version of this, called counterfactual fairness. The question it asks is beautifully direct: would this exact individual have gotten the same decision if they had belonged to a different group, holding everything else about them fixed? That's an intervention, in the do-calculus sense, run at the level of a single person. If flipping their group would change the decision, then the model isn't counterfactually fair to them.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Koozner and colleagues, in 2017, gave us a sharp version of this, called counterfactual fairness. The question it asks is beautifully direct: would this exact individual have gotten the same decision if they had belonged to a different group, holding everything else about them fixed? That's an intervention, in the do-calculus sense, run at the level of a single person. If flipping their group would change the decision, then the model isn't counterfactually fair to them.

beats.label: Counterfactual fairness — tangent
beats.narration_text: This reads like a thought experiment. The left side is this exact person's decision in their actual group; the right side is their decision in a world where we flip their group and hold everything else fixed; and the equals sign demands those be the same. The arrow A-left-arrow-a means we intervene and set the group; U is everything personal we hold constant. So take a loan model that only looks at education — but historically, access to that education was shaped by race. Flip the person's race, and their credential changes too, so the decision changes. A race-blind model still treats them differently. The value, and it's a strong one: a counterfactual change of group should leave the decision untouched. Back to it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: This reads like a thought experiment. The left side is this exact person's decision in their actual group; the right side is their decision in a world where we flip their group and hold everything else fixed; and the equals sign demands those be the same. The arrow A-left-arrow-a means we intervene and set the group; U is everything personal we hold constant. So take a loan model that only looks at education — but historically, access to that education was shaped by race. Flip the person's race, and their credential changes too, so the decision changes. A race-blind model still treats them differently. The value, and it's a strong one: a counterfactual change of group should leave the decision untouched. Back to it.

beats.label: Counterfactual — example
beats.speaker_notes: Loan default. Race causally influences educational credential through historical access; credential predicts default. Changing race counterfactually changes the credential — so the prediction changes. Counterfactual fairness violated. Remedy: exclude variables downstream of A along illegitimate paths.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example A loan model that “only looks at education” can still encode race. Race (A) → Credential (E) → Predicted default Historical access to education was shaped by race. So if you counterfactually changed this applicant’s race and held everything else fixed, their credential would change — and the prediction with it. Counterfactual fairness is violated , even though “race” was never a feature. The remedy is to drop variables downstream of A along paths you judge illegitimate — and which paths are illegitimate is, again, a values question.
beats.narration_text: Here's how a model that only looks at education can still encode race. Historical access to education was itself shaped by race, so race flows into the credential, and the credential predicts default. Now run the counterfactual: change this applicant's race, hold everything else fixed, and their credential would change too, because of that historical pathway, so the prediction changes. Counterfactual fairness, violated, even though race was never an input. The remedy is to exclude variables that sit downstream of group along an illegitimate path.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Here's how a model that only looks at education can still encode race. Historical access to education was itself shaped by race, so race flows into the credential, and the credential predicts default. Now run the counterfactual: change this applicant's race, hold everything else fixed, and their credential would change too, because of that historical pathway, so the prediction changes. Counterfactual fairness, violated, even though race was never an input. The remedy is to exclude variables that sit downstream of group along an illegitimate path.

beats.label: Generalized Entropy Index
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider. Group metrics give a binary verdict. GE gives a continuous measure of how much unfairness exists — and decomposes it.
beats.on_slide_text: Part six The Generalized Entropy Index Not “is it fair, yes/no” — but how much unfairness, and where it comes from. Borrowed from income-inequality economics.
beats.narration_text: Part six. Every metric so far hands you a verdict: fair or not fair, a gap or no gap. But sometimes you want a dial, not a verdict. How much unfairness is there, and where is it coming from? For that, we borrow a tool from economics, the same family of indices used to measure income inequality.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Part six. Every metric so far hands you a verdict: fair or not fair, a gap or no gap. But sometimes you want a dial, not a verdict. How much unfairness is there, and where is it coming from? For that, we borrow a tool from economics, the same family of indices used to measure income inequality.

beats.label: GE Index — equation
beats.speaker_notes: GE index formula. Benefit b_i distributes across individuals the way income distributes. Special cases at alpha = 0 (MLD), 1 (Theil), 2 (half squared CV).
beats.on_slide_text: Equation · how much, and where A single number for total unfairness GE(\alpha) = \frac{1}{N\alpha(\alpha-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[\left(\frac{b_i}{\mu}\right)^{\alpha} - 1\right] α = 0 Mean Log Deviation α = 1 Theil index α = 2 ½ (σ/μ)² — common pick In plain terms b i is each person’s benefit or error burden; μ its mean. The α dial decides where in the distribution you look.
beats.narration_text: It's called the Generalized Entropy Index. The idea: give each individual a benefit, essentially how well the model treated them relative to what they deserved, and then measure how unequally that benefit is distributed across everyone, exactly the way economists measure how unequally income is spread. There's a parameter, alpha, that recovers specific famous indices: mean log deviation at zero, the Theil index at one, and at two something close to the squared coefficient of variation, which is a common default.
beats.tts_normalized_text: It's called the Generalized Entropy Index. The idea: give each individual a benefit, essentially how well the model treated them relative to what they deserved, and then measure how unequally that benefit is distributed across everyone, exactly the way economists measure how unequally income is spread. There's a parameter, alpha, that recovers specific famous indices: mean log deviation at zero, the Tile index at one, and at two something close to the squared coefficient of variation, which is a common default.

beats.label: Generalized Entropy Index — tangent
beats.narration_text: Don't let the formula scare you — read what it does. The left side is a single number for total unfairness. The right side measures how spread out each person's benefit is around the average — straight from income-inequality economics. Little b-i is each person's benefit, mu is the average, and the one to watch is alpha, the exponent. Alpha is a knob: take the same spread of benefit and read it at alpha equals zero versus alpha equals two — a low alpha makes the index scream about the worst-served at the bottom, a high alpha responds more to the top. So picking alpha is itself a value choice about whose harm the number cares about most. That's the entropy index. Back to it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Don't let the formula scare you — read what it does. The left side is a single number for total unfairness. The right side measures how spread out each person's benefit is around the average — straight from income-inequality economics. Little b-i is each person's benefit, mu is the average, and the one to watch is alpha, the exponent. Alpha is a knob: take the same spread of benefit and read it at alpha equals zero versus alpha equals two — a low alpha makes the index scream about the worst-served at the bottom, a high alpha responds more to the top. So picking alpha is itself a value choice about whose harm the number cares about most. That's the entropy index. Back to it.

beats.label: GE — alpha shifts attention
beats.speaker_notes: The alpha parameter shifts attention across the distribution. Low alpha weights inequity at the bottom; high alpha at the top. Real-world: choose alpha to match whose harm you most want to catch.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example · choosing α α decides whose harm you most want to catch. Same distribution of benefit, five settings of α. A low α makes the index scream about the worst-served tail ; a high α responds more to the top. Picking α is itself a values choice about who you’re protecting.
beats.narration_text: That alpha isn't a technicality, it's a values knob. A low alpha makes the index scream about the worst-served people, the bottom of the distribution. A high alpha makes it more responsive to the top. Same distribution of benefit, different alpha, different number, because you are choosing whose harm you most want the metric to notice. Picking alpha is itself a moral decision about who you're trying to protect.
beats.tts_normalized_text: That alpha isn't a technicality, it's a values knob. A low alpha makes the index scream about the worst-served people, the bottom of the distribution. A high alpha makes it more responsive to the top. Same distribution of benefit, different alpha, different number, because you are choosing whose harm you most want the metric to notice. Picking alpha is itself a moral decision about who you're trying to protect.

beats.label: GE — decomposition
beats.speaker_notes: The decomposition: total = within-group + between-group. A group audit catches only the between-group term. The ratio tells you the remedy.
beats.on_slide_text: The decomposition · its best trick Total unfairness = within-group + between-group GE(\alpha) = \underbrace{\sum_{g}\frac{n_g}{N}\left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu}\right)^{\alpha} GE_g(\alpha)}_{\text{within-group}} \;+\; \underbrace{GE(\alpha)\big|_{\text{between-group}}}_{\text{between-group}}
beats.narration_text: And here's its best trick. The total unfairness splits cleanly into two pieces: the part that lives between groups, and the part that lives within them. A standard group audit only ever sees the between-group term. It's completely blind to inequity happening inside a group. And the ratio of those two pieces actually points you toward the remedy: whether your problem is across groups, or hiding inside one.
beats.tts_normalized_text: And here's its best trick. The total unfairness splits cleanly into two pieces: the part that lives between groups, and the part that lives within them. A standard group audit only ever sees the between-group term. It's completely blind to inequity happening inside a group. And the ratio of those two pieces actually points you toward the remedy: whether your problem is across groups, or hiding inside one.

beats.label: The defense as deliverable
beats.speaker_notes: Section divider. The deliverable is not a number. It is a defended choice.
beats.on_slide_text: Part seven The defense is the deliverable The toolkit lets you implement a choice. It does not make the choice for you — or absolve you of defending it.
beats.narration_text: Part seven, and this is where the whole chapter comes to rest. Everything I've handed you so far is machinery, and machinery is seductive, because it feels like an answer. But notice what it actually does and doesn't do for you. It can carry out a decision you've already made. It cannot make that decision, and it won't stand up and justify it when someone asks you why. That justification, written down and owned by you, is the real thing you ship. Not the metric. The argument for the metric.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Part seven, and this is where the whole chapter comes to rest. Everything I've handed you so far is machinery, and machinery is seductive, because it feels like an answer. But notice what it actually does and doesn't do for you. It can carry out a decision you've already made. It cannot make that decision, and it won't stand up and justify it when someone asks you why. That justification, written down and owned by you, is the real thing you ship. Not the metric. The argument for the metric.

beats.label: The toolkit
beats.speaker_notes: Three families of debiasing tools: pre-, in-, and post-processing. None resolves the impossibility theorem — they implement a choice within it, and none reaches upstream structural bias.
beats.on_slide_text: What the toolkit can — and can’t — do Three families. Each implements a choice; none resolves the theorem. Pre-processing Reweight, resample, transform the training data before fitting. + model-agnostic, upstream − can’t reach mis-recorded truth In-processing Add fairness as a constraint or penalty in the training objective. + baked into the representation − accuracy trade-off locked in Post-processing Adjust thresholds or scores after training, per group. + applies to a frozen model − per-group thresholds invite legal objection None of them resolve the impossibility theorem, or reach upstream structural bias in the data-generating process.
beats.narration_text: Quick tour of what you can actually reach for. Three families. Pre-processing: reweight or resample the training data before you fit, model-agnostic and upstream, but it can't fix truth that was mis-recorded. In-processing: bake fairness into the objective as a constraint while training. Post-processing: adjust the outputs or thresholds after the fact. All useful, but notice none of them resolves the impossibility theorem. They implement a choice within it. And none of them reaches the upstream, structural bias that produced the data in the first place.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Quick tour of what you can actually reach for. Three families. Pre-processing: reweight or resample the training data before you fit, model-agnostic and upstream, but it can't fix truth that was mis-recorded. In-processing: bake fairness into the objective as a constraint while training. Post-processing: adjust the outputs or thresholds after the fact. All useful, but notice none of them resolves the impossibility theorem. They implement a choice within it. And none of them reaches the upstream, structural bias that produced the data in the first place.

beats.label: The defended choice
beats.speaker_notes: The six-box scaffold. You will produce something in this form on every fairness deliverable: specify, compute, name the conflict, state the choice, defend in writing, name what would change your mind.
beats.on_slide_text: The deliverable, structured A defended choice — six boxes, every time.
beats.narration_text: So here's the form your work should take, every single time. Six boxes. Specify the metric you're choosing. Compute it. Name the conflict, say out loud which criterion you're giving up. State your choice. Defend it in writing, tied to the deployment. And, the box people love to skip, name what would change your mind. If you can fill these six honestly, you've done fairness engineering. If you can't, you've just computed a number.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So here's the form your work should take, every single time. Six boxes. Specify the metric you're choosing. Compute it. Name the conflict, say out loud which criterion you're giving up. State your choice. Defend it in writing, tied to the deployment. And, the box people love to skip, name what would change your mind. If you can fill these six honestly, you've done fairness engineering. If you can't, you've just computed a number.

beats.label: The persistent limits
beats.speaker_notes: What fairness metrics cannot do. Group, individual, causal — each requires something the others don't, and none can tell you whether the prediction task itself is fair to formulate.
beats.on_slide_text: A clean statement of the limits What no fairness metric can do for you. Group metrics can’t tell you two similar individuals are treated consistently. Individual fairness needs a similarity metric d that encodes values. Causal fairness needs a structural model the data alone can’t supply. None tells you whether the prediction task is fair to formulate . A model can ace re-arrest metrics — but re-arrest is not the construct society cares about. None tells you whether affected people consider it fair — procedural fairness, fairness of opportunity, participation in the design all sit outside the metrics. These are limits on the metrics, not failures of them. The supervisory work continues past the metric — into the construct, the deployment, and the participation.
beats.narration_text: Let me be honest about the ceiling. Group metrics can't certify that two similar individuals were treated consistently. Individual fairness needs a similarity metric that someone has to load with values. Causal fairness needs a structural model the data simply can't give you. Each approach needs something the others can't supply, and not one of them can tell you whether the prediction task itself was even fair to pose in the first place. The tools have a hard edge, and you should know exactly where it is.

beats.narration_text: So the supervisory move, the thing I want you walking out with: understand the math, choose the values, and defend the integration. Three formally distinct metrics can be flatly incompatible on a single dataset the moment base rates differ. That's structural, and it isn't going away. What's yours to own is connecting the metric you chose to the deployment, and to the people who bear the cost of being wrong. Next time, we turn from fairness to a different kind of pressure on your model: adversarial robustness.
